coredata.dc:title: The ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on the mixture of CdTe quantum dots and graphene quantum dots for quantitative analysis of silver in drinks
coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Li
Li, Lan
Zeng, Xiaodan
Yu, Shihua
Zhang, Jianpo
coredata.dc:description: Silver nanoparticles can be used in antibacterial packaging or disinfection. Research has shown that sugary fluid induces the leaching of silver nanoparticles into water, which may be harmful to humans. Single wavelength fluorescence analysis has been used for quantitative analysis of silver nanoparticles but suffers from low specificity and poor anti-interference ability. In this paper, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor system (GCS) was used for the detection of Ag+, which realized both visual detection and quantitative analysis of silver in drinks. The color changes of GCS with different concentrations of Ag+ are distinguishable and easy to analyze. There is also a good linear relationship between the concentrations of Ag+ and varieties of F424 nm/F570 nm, and the lowest detection limit reached 0.2266 nmol/L. This GCS shows good selectivity and recovery and could be used for the detection of Ag+ in drink samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence
Colorimetric fluorescence analysis
Ag+
Quantitative analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CdTe QDs and GGQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: department of science and technology of Jilin Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology of Jilin Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We greatly appreciate the financial support from the projects of NSFC (No. 22106051) and department of science and technology of Jilin Province (20220203020SF). HRTEM and XRD data were obtained using equipment maintained by Jilin Insititute of Chemical Technology Center of Characterization and Analysis. The authors would like to thank Linghui Bu from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the TEM analysis. The asynchronous 2D correlation fluorescence spectra were obtained using 2Dshige (c) Shigeaki Morita, Kwansei-Gakuin University, 2004-2005.
We greatly appreciate the financial support from the projects of NSFC (No. 22106051) and department of science and technology of Jilin Province (20220203020SF).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (A), XPS (B), UV absorbance spectra (C) and fluorescence spectra (D) of GQDs and GGQDs.
Fluorescence spectra (A) and quenching efficiency (B) of GCS with different concentrations of Ag+.
The relationship of concentrations of Ag+ to F424 nm/F570 nm and log (F424 nm/F570 nm) (A). The relationship of concentrations of Ag+ to F424 nm/F570 nm (B).
GCS with different of selected interferences.
The analytical results for Ag+ in sample.

head.ce:title: The ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on the mixture of CdTe quantum dots and graphene quantum dots for quantitative analysis of silver in drinks

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Jilin Institute of Chemical Technology, Jilin 132022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Jilin Institute of Chemical Technology, Jilin 132022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Silver nanoparticles can be used in antibacterial packaging or disinfection. Research has shown that sugary fluid induces the leaching of silver nanoparticles into water, which may be harmful to humans. Single wavelength fluorescence analysis has been used for quantitative analysis of silver nanoparticles but suffers from low specificity and poor anti-interference ability. In this paper, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor system (GCS) was used for the detection of Ag+, which realized both visual detection and quantitative analysis of silver in drinks. The color changes of GCS with different concentrations of Ag+ are distinguishable and easy to analyze. There is also a good linear relationship between the concentrations of Ag+ and varieties of F424 nm/F570 nm, and the lowest detection limit reached 0.2266 nmol/L. This GCS shows good selectivity and recovery and could be used for the detection of Ag+ in drink samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Applying silver nanoparticles to antibacterial packaging or disinfection for space, equipment pipes and packaging containers is a new area of research. Yang (Yang et al., 2021) reported that silver nanoparticles in antibacterial packaging might enter food or drinks. Compared with water alone, sugary fluid containing starch, citrate, and fat would lead to more silver nanoparticle leachates. Silver nanoparticles accumulate in the human body, pass through the mucosal barrier and enter the body, which results in cell damage.

A research group of the EFSA Panel on Food Contact Materials, Enzymes, and Processing Aids (CEP) has evaluated the security of silver nanoparticles added to plastic. Panel (EFSA et al., 2014) noted that silver nanoparticles might cluster together or aggregate, but the morphology of particles remained unchanged and could not move or release into food. Silver ions in food are below 6 μg/kg, which is lower than the limit of 50 μg/kg mooted by the AFC group in 2004. Therefore, Ag+ adopted in polyolefins, polyesters, and styrene (up to 0.025% w/w) does not raise safety concerns for the consumer. Unrestricted usage of silver ions would be harmful to humans, and it is particularly important for quantitative analysis of silver ions in food or water.

In recent years, a variety of silver ion detection methods have been developed, such as nucleic acid aptamer detection, potentiometric methods (Balamurugan et al., 2022), electrochemical analysis, atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) (Pohl, 2009), fluorescence analysis (Li et al., 2011; Qi et al., 2017; Arulraj et al., 2015), etc. Aetc. Among them, fluorescence analysis with obvious advantages of high sensitivity, low sample demand, strong selectivity, and low cost has been widely studied. Among fluorescence analyses, quantum dots with good fluorescence emission properties have been widely used for the detection of substances based on fluorescence quenching (Jin et al., 2017), enhancement (Chandan et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2012), redshift of wavelength (Li et al., 2014; Pu et al., 2021) and fluorescence energy transfer (Feng et al., 2016) of quantum dots adopted by analytes.

According to the characteristics of the emission wavelength of the materials used, fluorescence analysis can be divided into the single wavelength fluorescence method and ratiometric fluorescence method, multiwavelength analysis, and other analysis types. Single wavelength fluorescence analysis has the advantages of simple operation and high detection sensitivity but suffers from numerous factors, including low specificity and poor anti-interference ability (Qu et al., 2013; Yan et al., 2015), the surrounding environment, light source intensity noise, probe concentration, built-in correction for background interference (Shangguan et al., 2016; Xia et al., 2019), and other factors, which can be effectively reduced by the ratiometric fluorescence system. In a ratiometric fluorescence system, there are two fluorescence emission peaks that come from a single (Chen et al., 2019) or two fluorescent materials (Li et al., 2021; Lu et al., 2021; Qiu et al., 2022), which are used as signal and reference channels, respectively. Compared with single wavelength fluorescence analysis, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor also has significant advantages in sensitivity and accuracy (Yan et al., 2021), which are very important for quantitative analysis. Ratiometric fluorescence sensors have been widely studied in recent years.

In this study, glutathione graphene quantum dots (GGQDs) were synthesized from glutathione and citric acid, and then GGQDs were mixed with CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs) to construct a ratio fluorescence sensor system (GCS). The interaction between silver ions and GCS has been discussed in detail, which shows that this GCS could be used for quantitative and colorimetric fluorescent analysis of silver in drinks.

3-Mercaptopropyl acid (MPA) (99+%), tellurium powder (∼200 mesh, 99.8%), CdCl2 (99+%), NaBH4 (99%) were from Aldrich Chemical Co. Silver nitrate and citric acid were from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Sodium hydroxide was obtained from Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. l-Glutathione reduced was obtained from Shanghai Maclin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. All other agents were of analytical reagent grade and used as received. The water used throughout was doubly distilled water (>18 MΩ·cm).

The fluorescence spectra were measured on an F-280 fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a xenon lamp and quartz carrier at room temperature with an excitation and emission slit at 5.0 nm. The fluorescence decay curve was obtained using Fluro Max-Plus. Transmission electron micrographs were recorded with a TF20 electron microscope. The XPS spectra were recorded with a Scientific K-Alpha. The Raman spectrum was recorded with a BWS465-532S. Fluorescence photographs were recorded using a Huawei Mate 30 telephone. UV absorption spectra were measured on a Shimadzu UV-2550 UV–visible spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CdTe QDs and GGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: CdTe QDs were prepared in the aqueous phase by two reaction steps according to the method we have reported (Zhang et al., 2017). GGQDs powder was prepared by a modified method (Dong et al., 2012; He et al., 2014). Citric acid (1.0 g) and l-glutathione reduced mixture (the mass ratio is 9:1) were added into the flask and heated directly for approximately 5 min on the electric stove until they turned light yellow. Then, 30 ml of sodium hydroxide solution (0.3 mol/L) was added into the flask under ultrasonication. The as-prepared solution was condensed by rotary evaporation and then dried at 80 °C for 2 h, and a light-yellow powder was obtained.

The concentration of silver standard solution was 0.04 mg/mL, which was diluted for different analyses. The fluorescence emission spectra of GCS with different concentrations of Ag+ were tested after incubating for 2 min (pH = 8, adjusted by 0.1 mol/L NaOH) at room temperature. The total volume of GCS with Ag+ was 2 ml, the excitation wavelength was 350 nm, and the fluorescence emission wavelengths were 424 and 570 nm.

The TEM image (Fig. 1 A) shows that the GGQDs were nearly spherical with good dispersion, and the interplanar distance of the crystalline lattice plane was 0.232 nm, which is similar to that of GQDs. Elements are analyzed by XPS spectra (Fig. 1B). Four characteristic peaks correspond to S, C, O and N, of which atomic percentages are 2.64%, 37.98%, 52.14%, and 7.25%, respectively. Surface groups are analyzed by deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectra (as shown in Fig. S1). There are three fitting peaks in the XPS spectra of S2p (Fig. S1A), corresponding to R-SO-R′ (165.3 eV), 2P3/2 (164.5 eV) and R1-S-R (163.5 eV). There are five fitting peaks in the XPS spectra of C1s (Fig. S1B), corresponding to CO3 2– (289.4 eV), COOR (288.9 eV), OC-O (288.6 eV), CN (286.4 eV) and C-OR (285.4 eV). There are two fitting peaks in the XPS spectra of N1s (Fig. S1C), corresponding to graphitic-N (401.6 eV) and –NH- (400.1 eV). There are two fitting peaks in the XPS spectra of O1s (Fig. S1D), corresponding to CO (533.3 eV) and COH (532.7 eV). All of the above results indicated that there are sulfhydryl, carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl groups on the surface of the product.

The Raman spectrum shows that two peaks of GGQDs (Fig. S2), corresponding to the D band at ∼1408 cm−1 and the G band at ∼1640 cm−1, are the characteristic peaks of graphene. The band intensity ID/IG (0.5131) is also similar to that of graphene (0.5832), which indicates that doping of l-glutathione reduced in GGQDs has little effect on the surface defects of graphene (Jian et al., 2016). The absorbance and fluorescence spectra of GGQDs and GQDs (synthesized without glutathione under the same conditions as a controlled experiment) are shown in Fig. 1C and D. Compared with GQDs, both the maximum absorbance and fluorescence wavelength are blue shifted, which may be because the diameter of GGQDs has decreased. As shown in Fig. 1C, for GGQDs, except for the peak at approximately 340 nm, there is a significant peak at approximately 550 nm. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared GGQDs (Fig. 1D) was significantly higher than that of the GQDs, which indicates that doping of l-glutathione reduced in GGQDs could improve the fluorescence emission properties. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S3, the weighted average method was used to calculate the average fluorescence decay time of GGQDs (τ 0 = 10.74 ns) (Gore et al., 2013), which is slightly higher than that of GQDs (6.14 ns). This may be used to explain why the fluorescence intensity of GGQDs is higher than that of GQDs. Above all, GGQDs have been successfully synthesized.

As shown in Fig. S4, a hump spectrum without overlapping with each other could be observed after mixing CdTe QDs with GGQDs. Both the intensity ratios of GGQDs and CdTe QDs (F424 nm/F570 nm) before and after mixing are approximately 0.5, indicating that this GGQDs and CdTe QDs mixture could be applied as a ratiometric fluorescence sensor system (GCS).

To obtain more valuable results, CdTe quantum dots with a maximum fluorescence emission wavelength of 570 nm were selected, of which the color is yellow under a 365 nm UV lamp. Under a 365 nm UV lamp, the color of single GGQDs is blue, and the color of GCS is yellow-green. As shown in Fig. 2 , when different concentrations of Ag+ were introduced into GCS, both the fluorescence intensity at 424 nm and 570 nm decreased. The quenching efficiency of CdTe QDs (570 nm) is obviously faster than that of GGQDs (at 424 nm). The maximum quenching efficiency of CdTe QDs reached 93.31%, while the maximum quenching efficiency of GGQDs was 10.76%. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F424 nm/F570 nm) increases, and the color of GCS changes under a 365 nm UV lamp. As shown in Fig. 2A insert, the original color of GCS is yellow-green. When different concentrations of Ag+ are introduced (from 0 to 7.41 μmol/L), the color of GCS changes from yellow-green to orange, pink, purple, and blue. These obvious color changes make it easier to observe, which could be used for colorimetric fluorescence detection of Ag+. Compared with other colorimetric fluorescence methods (Song et al., 2022), this method is more visible, and the fusion image is comfortable for human eyes.

To study why the color of GCS changed, different concentrations of Ag+ were added to CdTe QDs, the fluorescence spectra (Fig. S5A) were analyzed, and the normalized fluorescence emission spectra are shown in Fig. S6(A-C). The results show that the maximum fluorescence emission wavelengths slightly redshift when 0.82–2.71 µmol/L Ag+ was added (Fig. S6A). Then, fluorescence emission peaks shifted to the side of short waves when 3.18–7.41 µmol/L Ag+ was added (Fig. S6B - C). However, the edge of the fluorescence emission spectrum remains redshifted compared with that of single CdTe quantum dots. There may be a new fluorescence peak overlapping with the fluorescence peak of CdTe quantum dots.

The asynchronous 2D correlation fluorescence spectra could be used to distinguish peaks from different origins that overlap together. Two close-set peaks could be distinguished if there were significant continuous variations in fluorescence intensity. As shown in Fig. S6D, asynchronous 2D correlation fluorescence spectra were used to analyze Fig. S5A. The results show that there are two fluorescence emission peaks at approximately 570 nm and 625 nm. The peak at approximately 570 nm corresponds to CdTe quantum dots. The peak at approximately 625 nm may correspond to Ag2S. Because the Ksp of CdS is 8 × 10-27 and the Ksp of Ag2S is 2 × 10-49, Ag could replace Cd to form Ag2S with MPA. The deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectra of S 2p of CdTe quantum dots Ag+ (Fig. S6B) could be used to verify this hypothesis. The Ag-S-Ag peak at 162.2 eV indicates the formation of Ag2S. The peak at approximately 625 nm shows red color under UV light.

When different concentrations of Ag+ were added into GCS, the stability of CdTe quantum dots decreased, which induced a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of CdTe quantum dots (yellow color). Along with the fluorescence emission of GGQDs (blue color) and Ag2S (red color), the color of GCS changes from yellow green to blue.

As shown in Fig. S7A, when Ag+ was added to GCS, the higher the concentration of Ag+ was, the longer the time needed to reach equilibrium. A pseudo-second-order kinetics model (Lian et al., 2014) is used to analyze the data (Formula 2 is obtained from Formula 1), as shown in Fig. S7B, where time t is taken as the abscissa and t/F424 nm/F570 nm as the ordinate. The fitting lines have a good linear relationship, and the R2 values of the fitting lines are 0.995, 0.998, and 0.999, which fit well with the law of the pseudo-second-order kinetics model. This is because there is a mercapto group in CdTe QDs, and Ag+ could be adsorbed on the CdTe QDs to form AgS. The quantity of Ag+ increases on the CdTe QDs, along with the time passed by, which would induce quenching of CdTe QDs until reaching equilibrium. As the amount of Ag+ added was below 0.2 mg/L, to obtain reliable results, 2 min was selected as the bathing time.

1
k 2

The effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of GCS with Ag+ has been adopted, as shown in Fig. S8. The results show that pH has little effect on the fluorescence intensity of GGQDs. The fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs first increased and then decreased with increasing pH, so pH = 8 was selected for further study.

For quantitative analysis of Ag+, fluorescence spectra of GCS with different concentrations of Ag+ (0.5887–5.298 μmol/L) were obtained, and the corresponding data analysis results are shown in Fig. 3 A, which show a rising curve deviating from the X-axis. This could be explained as follows: When too much Ag+ (more than 0.14 μmol/L) is present, an optical collision occurs between the molecules of GCS and Ag+, and then Ag+ can be absorbed on the CdTe QDs. The results indicate that the quenching of GCS adopted by Ag+ involves both dynamic and static quenching processes (Zhang et al., 2017). Additionally, there is a good linear relationship between the concentrations of Ag+ and log (F424 nm/F570 nm) (Fig. 3A insert), which could be used for the quantitative analysis of Ag+. The linear regression equation is log(F424 nm/F570 nm) = 0.3278 [Ag+] −0.5777, and the corresponding R2 is 0.998. The detection limit was 0.1430 μmol/L.

When the concentrations of Ag+ are below 0.14 μmol/L, such as when the concentrations of Ag+ are 0.04720–0.1180 μmol/L (Fig. 3B), there is a good linear relationship between the concentrations of Ag+ and F424 nm/F570 nm, and the linear regression equation is F424 nm/F570 nm = 7.708 [Ag+] + 0.8736, corresponding to an R2 of 0.997. The detection limit was 0.004679 μmol/L. This may be because the concentrations of Ag+ are too low to collide with the molecules of GCS, and Ag+ has been directly adsorbed on the CdTe QDs.

The mechanism could be stated as follows: As stated by Zhang (Zhang, et al., 2017), the substance that interacts with fluorescent molecules and causes a decrease in fluorescence intensity is called a fluorescence quencher. This quenching process can be divided into dynamic quenching and static quenching processes. The interaction between the quencher and the ground state molecule of the fluorescent substance to form a complex is a static quenching process. Due to the formation of the complex, the UV-absorbance spectra of fluorescent molecules will change before and after adding the quencher. The UV–visible absorption spectra of GCS with and without Ag+ (Fig. S9A) indicate that static quenching occurs. As shown in Fig. S9B, the average fluorescence lifetimes of CdTe quantum dots before and after adding Ag+ are 20 ns (τ0) and 19.98 (τ) ns, respectively. τ0/τ ≈ 1 shows that the lifetimes of CdTe quantum dots remain unchanged with or without Ag+, which is another characteristic of the static quenching progress. The dynamic fluorescence quenching rate constant Kq could be calculated by Kq = K/τ0, where K is the slope from Fig. 3B and τ0 is the average fluorescence lifetime of CdTe QDs. Kq = 3.854 × 1014 mol−1·s−1 could be obtained, which is significantly larger than the maximum dynamic quenching constant (2 × 1010 mol−1·s−1), and it is also one of the features of the static quenching process. Above all, we could conclude that it is a static quenching process of the CdTe quantum dots adopted by Ag+ when the concentration of Ag+ added in GCS is below 0.14 μmol/L.

A similar conclusion could also be obtained, as shown in Fig. S10. When the concentration of Ag+ was 1.177–5.887 nmol/L, the linear regression equation was F424 nm/F570 nm = 0.1433 [Ag+] + 1.603, and the corresponding R2 was 0.9978. The lowest detection limit is 0.2266 nmol/L, calculated from the equation (Gore et al., 2013) LOD = (3.3σ/k), where σ is the standard deviation of the y-intercepts of the regression lines and k is the slope of the calibration graph. Additionally, compared with other fluorescence methods (Table S1), this ratiometric fluorescence probe method has a lower detection limit.

Some metal ions and potential disruptors have been added to GCS to evaluate the selectivity of this GCS. As shown in Fig. 4 , Fe3+ and Fe2+ show remarkable fluorescence signal changes, and triethanolamine (without interference) is added into the real sample before detection, which could coordinate with some metal ions. The metal ions above are also added into a single GGQDs and CdTe QDs system as a control, which shows that this GCS sensor has clear superiority in selectivity compared with a single wavelength sensor (single GGQDs or CdTe QDs). Furthermore, this GCS system has good stability when it is exposed to continuous light excitation and high ionic strength. All results above indicate that this GCS system could be used for real sample detection.

Standard addition was adopted to evaluate the recovery of this GCS sensor. Mineral water and drink samples (an orange flavored carbonated drinks) obtained from the supermarket were selected as samples. Before analysis, a 10 ml drink sample was boiled for 20 min to remove CO2, and then NaOH was added to adjust the pH to 8. Then, the drink and mineral samples were tested using GCS, and no obvious fluorescence spectral change was detected, indicating that there was no Ag+ or that the concentration of Ag+ was too low to be detected. Then, water samples spiked with different concentrations of Ag+ were tested using this GCS. The results in Table 1 show that the recovery ranges are 97.85%–100.6% with relative standard deviations (RSDs) below 3.5%, such results indicate that this ratiometric fluorescence sensor could be used for quantitative analysis of water samples.

A ratiometric fluorescence system based on GGQDs and CdTe quantum dots has been successfully designed and applied for the detection of Ag+ in drinking water. Interestingly, this system not only could be used for quantitative analysis of Ag+ by using fluorescence spectra but could also realize naked-eye visible detection of high concentrations of Ag+ through color change under UV light. This significant color change will be useful for developing test kits or examination indicator papers. Furthermore, the lowest detection limit of GCS could reach 0.2266 nmol/L, which has significant advantages compared with other fluorescence detection methods. All results indicated that this ratiometric sensor is applicable.

Li Jin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Lan Li: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Xiaodan Zeng: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Shihua Yu: Data curation. Jianpo Zhang: .

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We greatly appreciate the financial support from the projects of NSFC (No. 22106051) and department of science and technology of Jilin Province (20220203020SF).

HRTEM and XRD data were obtained using equipment maintained by Jilin Insititute of Chemical Technology Center of Characterization and Analysis. The authors would like to thank Linghui Bu from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the TEM analysis. The asynchronous 2D correlation fluorescence spectra were obtained using 2Dshige (c) Shigeaki Morita, Kwansei-Gakuin University, 2004-2005.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.136926.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
1
